rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical vicinal fluoroselenenylation of alkenes using diaryl diselenides and Et3N·3HF to give β-fluoroselenides.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of diaryl diselenide (ArSeSeAr) to its radical cation, which fragments into an ArSe• radical and an ArSe+ cation; the alkene-derived carbon radical is then further oxidized at the anode to a selenonium cation that is trapped by fluoride.
electron_flow: Anode oxidizes ArSeSeAr to its radical cation, which fragments to ArSe• + ArSe+; ArSe• adds across the alkene to give a β-selenyl C-radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a selenonium (seleniranium) cation, and fluoride from Et3N·3HF opens it to deliver the β-fluoroselenide.

probe_reagents.name: (1-(2-phenylcyclopropyl)-vinyl)benzene (radical clock 29)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened product 30 obtained in 74% yield, indicating involvement of a selenium radical adding to the alkene.

probe_reagents.name: Dark control (electrolysis without light)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction still proceeded in >99% yield, excluding photochemical generation of ArSe• and supporting anodic oxidation as the radical source.

probe_reagents.name: No-electricity control
probe_reagents.observation: No product formed, confirming electrochemical requirement.

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1 and 2
probe_reagents.observation: Oxidation peaks: α-methylstyrene 2.48 V vs SCE; PhSeSePh 1.91 V vs SCE — diselenide is oxidized first under electrolysis.

notes: Reductive homo-coupling of the ArSe+ cation at the cathode is proposed to regenerate ArSeSeAr, closing a redox loop. An alternative photochemical pathway (visible-light homolysis of PhSeSePh to ArSe•) was explicitly excluded by the dark experiment.
